does: This paper introduces Triple Preference Optimization (TPO), a single-step preference learning method that optimizes over three preference signals simultaneously to improve both instruction-following and reasoning capabilities in large language models. Unlike Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which relies on a binary preference framework, TPO leverages additional preference information to create a more stable and robust optimization objective. The method is evaluated against DPO and its variants across comprehensive benchmarks using models like Mistral and Llama 3, demonstrating improvements in both chat-based tasks and reasoning-focused evaluations while maintaining stability across varying dataset sizes and noise levels.
connects: This work builds directly on the preference optimization line of research that moved away from reinforcement learning based approaches. It responds to limitations identified in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which simplified RLHF by eliminating the explicit reward modeling stage but traded off reasoning performance for instruction-following gains. The paper acknowledges prior modifications to DPO and extends this research direction by incorporating additional preference signals beyond binary comparisons. This connects to broader work on aligning language models through human feedback and the tension between different evaluation dimensions in model capability, drawing on recent understanding that preference learning methods can exhibit trade-offs between different ability types.
enables: By demonstrating that multi-signal preference optimization can simultaneously improve reasoning and instruction-following in a single training step, TPO opens pathways for more balanced alignment approaches. This work could enable future methods that incorporate even richer preference structures or integrate signals from multiple evaluation dimensions. The improved robustness to dataset noise and size variation suggests potential for more practical deployment in real-world scenarios with imperfect preference data. Furthermore, the framework could extend to other domains beyond language modeling where multi-objective preference learning is needed, and it may inspire research into understanding fundamental trade-offs between different model capabilities during preference-based fine-tuning.
